  • Related Topics

Dijkstra’s Algorithm

Overview

Dijkstra’s algorithm is a greedy algorithm that solves the single-source shortest path problem for a graph with NON-NEGATIVE edge weights. It finds the shortest path from a starting node (source) to all other nodes in the graph.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity: O((V + E) log V) with binary heap, O(V²) with array
  • Space Complexity: O(V) for distance array and visited set
  • Core Idea: Greedily select the unvisited node with minimum distance
  • When to Use: Single-source shortest path with non-negative weights
  • Limitation: Cannot handle negative edge weights

Core Characteristics

  • Greedy Algorithm: Always selects the minimum distance node
  • Priority Queue: Uses min-heap for efficient minimum extraction
  • Relaxation: Updates distances when shorter paths are found
  • Finalization: Once visited, a node’s distance is optimal

References

  • Dijkstra’s Algorithm Visualization
  • CP Algorithms - Dijkstra
  • Bellman-Ford Cheatsheet - For negative weight handling comparison
  • Floyd-Warshall Cheatsheet - For all-pairs shortest path comparison

Problem Categories

Category 1: Classic Shortest Path

  • Description: Standard single-source shortest path problems
  • Examples: LC 743 (Network Delay), LC 1514 (Path with Max Probability)
  • Pattern: Direct application of Dijkstra’s algorithm

Category 2: Shortest Path with Constraints

  • Description: Shortest path with additional restrictions (stops, cost limits)
  • Examples: LC 787 (Cheapest Flights K Stops), LC 1928 (Minimum Cost K Waypoints)
  • Pattern: Modified Dijkstra with state tracking

Category 3: Grid-based Shortest Path

  • Description: Finding optimal paths in 2D grids
  • Examples: LC 64 (Minimum Path Sum), LC 1631 (Path Min Effort), LC 778 (Swim in Rising Water)
  • Pattern: Dijkstra on implicit graph (grid cells as nodes)
  • ⚠️ Special Note: LC 64 can use pure DP instead of Dijkstra (see below)

Category 4: Multi-Source Shortest Path

  • Description: Multiple starting points to find shortest paths
  • Examples: LC 2812 (Find Safest Path), LC 1162 (As Far from Land)
  • Pattern: Initialize multiple sources or use super source

Category 5: Time-Dependent Shortest Path

  • Description: Path costs change based on time or sequence
  • Examples: LC 2045 (Second Minimum Time), LC 882 (Reachable Nodes)
  • Pattern: Track time/state in priority queue

⚠️ Critical Decision: When to Use Dijkstra vs DP

LC 64 vs LC 1631: The dist[][] Question

Question: Do we really need dist[r][c] (tracking minimum cost to reach each cell) for Dijkstra? Or is pure DP enough?

Answer: It depends on movement directions:

LC 64: Minimum Path Sum ✅ Pure DP is Sufficient

1Movement: RIGHT only ↓ or DOWN only →
  • Why DP works: You can only reach cell (i,j) from (i-1,j) or (i,j-1)
  • No need for dist[][]: Each cell is computed exactly once in topological order
  • No revisits: You can never find a “better path” after already computing a cell
  • Solution: Simple 2D DP or O(min(m,n)) space 1D DP
1// Pure DP - NO dist[][] needed
2public int minPathSum(int[][] grid) {
3    int m = grid.length, n = grid[0].length;
4    int[][] dp = new int[m][n];
5    dp[0][0] = grid[0][0];
6    
7    // First column: only from above
8    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++)
9        dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0] + grid[i][0];
10    
11    // First row: only from left
12    for (int j = 1; j < n; j++)
13        dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] + grid[0][j];
14    
15    // Fill rest
16    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++)
17        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++)
18            dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + Math.min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]);
19    
20    return dp[m-1][n-1];
21}
22// Time: O(m*n), Space: O(min(m,n))

LC 1631: Path With Minimum Effort ⚠️ Dijkstra + dist[][] Needed

1Movement: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT (all 4 directions)
  • Why Dijkstra needed: You might reach a cell from multiple paths, and later find a better path
  • dist[][] is essential: Tracks “best cost found so far” for each cell
  • Revisits possible: When moving in all 4 directions, you can revisit cells with better costs
  • Solution: Dijkstra with dist[][] + PriorityQueue
1// Dijkstra + dist[][] - NECESSARY for 4-directional movement
2public int minimumEffortPath(int[][] heights) {
3    int m = heights.length, n = heights[0].length;
4    
5    // dist[r][c] = minimum effort found so far to reach (r,c)
6    int[][] dist = new int[m][n];
7    for (int[] row : dist)
8        Arrays.fill(row, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
9    
10    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a[2] - b[2]);
11    pq.offer(new int[]{0, 0, 0});
12    dist[0][0] = 0;
13    
14    int[][] dirs = {{0,1}, {0,-1}, {1,0}, {-1,0}};
15    
16    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
17        int[] cur = pq.poll();
18        int r = cur[0], c = cur[1], cost = cur[2];
19        
20        // Already processed with better cost
21        if (cost > dist[r][c]) continue;
22        
23        if (r == m-1 && c == n-1) return cost;
24        
25        for (int[] d : dirs) {
26            int nr = r + d[0], nc = c + d[1];
27            if (nr >= 0 && nr < m && nc >= 0 && nc < n) {
28                int newCost = Math.max(cost, Math.abs(heights[nr][nc] - heights[r][c]));
29                if (newCost < dist[nr][nc]) {
30                    dist[nr][nc] = newCost;
31                    pq.offer(new int[]{nr, nc, newCost});
32                }
33            }
34        }
35    }
36    return -1;
37}
38// Time: O(m*n*log(m*n)), Space: O(m*n)

Summary Table

Problem Movement Cost Model Best Approach Need dist[][]? Need visited?
LC 64 Right + Down Additive sum 2D DP ❌ No ❌ No
LC 1631 4-directions Max of diffs Dijkstra ✅ Yes ✅ Yes (via dist check)
LC 1263 4-directions Additive cost Dijkstra ✅ Yes ✅ Yes (via dist check)

The dist[][] Purpose

1dist[r][c] = "What's the MINIMUM cost I've found SO FAR to reach (r,c)?"
  • Initialize: dist[r][c] = Integer.MAX_VALUE (unknown)
  • Update: When PQ pops a cell with cost C, check if (C > dist[r][c]) continue;
    • If true, we already found a better path → skip processing
    • This automatically prevents reprocessing without explicit visited array
  • Essential when: Multiple paths can reach the same cell → Dijkstra refinement needed

Dijkstra Implementation Variants for LC 1631

Variant 1: Using dist[][] Array (Recommended)

1// dist[r][c] stores minimum cost found so far to reach (r,c)
2public int minimumEffortPath(int[][] heights) {
3    int m = heights.length, n = heights[0].length;
4    int[][] dist = new int[m][n];
5    for (int[] row : dist) Arrays.fill(row, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
6    
7    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a[2] - b[2]);
8    pq.offer(new int[]{0, 0, 0}); // {row, col, effort}
9    dist[0][0] = 0;
10    
11    int[][] dirs = {{0,1}, {0,-1}, {1,0}, {-1,0}};
12    
13    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
14        int[] cur = pq.poll();
15        int r = cur[0], c = cur[1], effort = cur[2];
16        
17        // Destination check
18        if (r == m-1 && c == n-1) return effort;
19        
20        // Skip if already found better path
21        if (effort > dist[r][c]) continue;
22        
23        // Explore neighbors
24        for (int[] d : dirs) {
25            int nr = r + d[0], nc = c + d[1];
26            if (nr >= 0 && nr < m && nc >= 0 && nc < n) {
27                int nextEffort = Math.max(effort, Math.abs(heights[nr][nc] - heights[r][c]));
28                if (nextEffort < dist[nr][nc]) {
29                    dist[nr][nc] = nextEffort;
30                    pq.offer(new int[]{nr, nc, nextEffort});
31                }
32            }
33        }
34    }
35    return -1;
36}

Why it works: The dist[][] check if (effort > dist[r][c]) continue; automatically skips any path that’s worse than the best we’ve found.

Variant 2: Using visited[] Array

1// visited[] marks cells whose minimum effort is finalized
2public int minimumEffortPath_visited(int[][] heights) {
3    int m = heights.length, n = heights[0].length;
4    boolean[][] visited = new boolean[m][n];
5    
6    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
7    pq.offer(new int[]{0, 0, 0}); // {effort, row, col}
8    
9    int[][] dirs = {{0,1}, {0,-1}, {1,0}, {-1,0}};
10    
11    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
12        int[] cur = pq.poll();
13        int effort = cur[0], r = cur[1], c = cur[2];
14        
15        if (r == m-1 && c == n-1) return effort;
16        
17        // Once visited, we have minimum effort (thanks to min-heap)
18        if (visited[r][c]) continue;
19        visited[r][c] = true;
20        
21        for (int[] d : dirs) {
22            int nr = r + d[0], nc = c + d[1];
23            if (nr >= 0 && nr < m && nc >= 0 && nc < n && !visited[nr][nc]) {
24                int nextEffort = Math.max(effort, Math.abs(heights[nr][nc] - heights[r][c]));
25                pq.offer(new int[]{nextEffort, nr, nc});
26            }
27        }
28    }
29    return -1;
30}

Why visited works: The min-heap guarantees that the first time we pop a cell is with optimal effort, so marking it visited prevents reprocessing.

Variant Comparison

Approach Space Logic Best For
dist[][] Extra O(m×n) Compare against best known When updating multiple times
visited[] Extra O(m×n) Mark as finalized Simpler logic, faster exit

Alternative Approaches for LC 1631

Approach 3: Binary Search + DFS

1// Binary search on effort + DFS to check if reachable
2public int minimumEffortPath_binarySearch(int[][] heights) {
3    int lo = 0, hi = 1_000_000;
4    
5    while (lo < hi) {
6        int mid = (lo + hi) / 2;
7        if (canReach(heights, mid)) {
8            hi = mid;
9        } else {
10            lo = mid + 1;
11        }
12    }
13    return lo;
14}
15
16private boolean canReach(int[][] h, int limit) {
17    int m = h.length, n = h[0].length;
18    boolean[][] visited = new boolean[m][n];
19    return dfs(h, 0, 0, limit, visited);
20}
21
22private boolean dfs(int[][] h, int r, int c, int limit, boolean[][] visited) {
23    if (r < 0 || r >= h.length || c < 0 || c >= h[0].length || visited[r][c])
24        return false;
25    
26    visited[r][c] = true;
27    if (r == h.length-1 && c == h[0].length-1) return true;
28    
29    int[][] dirs = {{0,1}, {0,-1}, {1,0}, {-1,0}};
30    for (int[] d : dirs) {
31        int nr = r + d[0], nc = c + d[1];
32        if (nr >= 0 && nr < h.length && nc >= 0 && nc < h[0].length) {
33            if (Math.abs(h[nr][nc] - h[r][c]) <= limit && dfs(h, nr, nc, limit, visited)) {
34                return true;
35            }
36        }
37    }
38    return false;
39}

Time: O((V+E) × log(maxH)) | Space: O(V)

Approach 4: Union Find (Kruskal’s Algorithm)

1// Build graph as edges, sort by weight, union until src-dest connected
2public int minimumEffortPath_unionFind(int[][] heights) {
3    int m = heights.length, n = heights[0].length;
4    List<int[]> edges = new ArrayList<>();
5    
6    // Build all edges
7    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
8        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
9            if (i > 0) // edge down
10                edges.add(new int[]{i*n+j, (i-1)*n+j, Math.abs(heights[i][j]-heights[i-1][j])});
11            if (j > 0) // edge right
12                edges.add(new int[]{i*n+j, i*n+j-1, Math.abs(heights[i][j]-heights[i][j-1])});
13        }
14    }
15    
16    // Sort edges by effort (Kruskal's principle)
17    edges.sort((a, b) -> a[2] - b[2]);
18    
19    UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(m * n);
20    int src = 0, dst = m*n - 1;
21    
22    for (int[] edge : edges) {
23        uf.union(edge[0], edge[1]);
24        if (uf.find(src) == uf.find(dst)) {
25            return edge[2]; // Return effort when src-dst first connected
26        }
27    }
28    return 0;
29}
30
31class UnionFind {
32    int[] parent, rank;
33    UnionFind(int n) {
34        parent = new int[n];
35        rank = new int[n];
36        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = i;
37    }
38    
39    int find(int x) {
40        if (parent[x] != x) parent[x] = find(parent[x]);
41        return parent[x];
42    }
43    
44    void union(int x, int y) {
45        int px = find(x), py = find(y);
46        if (px == py) return;
47        if (rank[px] < rank[py]) { int t = px; px = py; py = t; }
48        parent[py] = px;
49        if (rank[px] == rank[py]) rank[px]++;
50    }
51}

Time: O((V+E) log(V+E)) = O(m×n × log(m×n)) | Space: O(m×n)

Approach Selection for LC 1631

Approach Pros Cons Best When
Dijkstra + dist[][] Most intuitive, standard Extra space Want classic Dijkstra pattern
Dijkstra + visited[] Simpler early termination Less flexible Just need minimum effort
Binary Search + DFS Uses less memory in some cases Slower (repeated DFS) Memory is critical
Union Find Elegant graph perspective Complex to implement Learning Union Find

⚠️ Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: Do I need BOTH dist[] AND visited[]?

A: No, you use ONE or the OTHER:

  • Option A: dist[][] → Check if (newCost < dist[r][c]) before processing
  • Option B: visited[] → Mark as visited after first pop from PQ

Both prevent reprocessing the same cell. Pick whichever feels clearer.

Q2: Why can’t I use DP for LC 1631 like I do for LC 64?

A: Because of movement direction:

  • LC 64: Only move RIGHT/DOWN → Topological order exists → DP works ✅
  • LC 1631: Can move UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT → Cycles exist → DP fails ❌

With 4-directional movement, you can have circular dependencies:

1(1,1) → (1,2) → (2,2) → (2,1) → (1,1)

DP requires dependencies to form a DAG (no cycles), so Dijkstra or Binary Search required.

Q3: What’s the difference between “cost” and “effort” in LC 1631?

A: They measure different things in different problems:

  • Cost (LC 64, 1263): Sum of all values along path = cost += value
  • Effort (LC 1631): Max difference between consecutive cells = effort = max(effort, |diff|)

Cost is additive; effort is not. This non-additivity is why DP fails.

Q4: When should I use Union Find instead of Dijkstra?

A: Use Union Find when:

  • You’re comfortable building explicit edge list
  • You want to see the problem as a graph connectivity problem
  • You’re practicing Kruskal’s algorithm

Both have same time complexity O(m×n×log(m×n)), but Dijkstra is usually more intuitive for grid problems.

Q5: Does dist[r][c] check work without explicit visited[]?

A: Yes! The check if (cost > dist[r][c]) continue; IS your visited mechanism:

  • First time we pop (r,c): cost == dist[r][c] → process
  • Later pops to (r,c): cost > dist[r][c] → skip (it’s like “already visited”)

So you get the benefit of visited[] semantics without an extra array.

Templates & Algorithms

Template Comparison Table

Template Type Use Case State Tracked When to Use
Basic Dijkstra Standard shortest path (distance, node) No constraints
Constrained Path Path with limits (cost, node, constraint) K stops, budget
Grid Dijkstra 2D grid navigation (cost, x, y) Matrix problems
Multi-Source Multiple starts (dist, node, source) Multiple origins
Time-Variant Time-dependent (time, node, state) Dynamic costs

Universal Dijkstra Template

1import heapq
2import collections
3
4def dijkstra(n, edges, src, dst):
5    # Build adjacency list
6    graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
7    for u, v, w in edges:
8        graph[u].append((v, w))
9    
10    # Min heap: (distance, node)
11    pq = [(0, src)]
12    # Distance array
13    dist = [float('inf')] * n
14    dist[src] = 0
15    # Visited set (optional but recommended)
16    visited = set()
17    
18    while pq:
19        d, u = heapq.heappop(pq)
20        
21        # Skip if already processed with better distance
22        if u in visited:
23            continue
24        visited.add(u)
25        
26        # Found destination
27        if u == dst:
28            return d
29        
30        # Relax edges
31        for v, w in graph[u]:
32            if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
33                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
34                heapq.heappush(pq, (dist[v], v))
35    
36    return dist[dst] if dist[dst] != float('inf') else -1

Template 1: Basic Dijkstra

1def dijkstra_basic(n, edges, src):
2    """Find shortest paths from src to all nodes"""
3    graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
4    for u, v, w in edges:
5        graph[u].append((v, w))
6    
7    dist = [float('inf')] * n
8    dist[src] = 0
9    pq = [(0, src)]  # (distance, node)
10    
11    while pq:
12        d, u = heapq.heappop(pq)
13        if d > dist[u]:  # Already processed
14            continue
15        
16        for v, w in graph[u]:
17            if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
18                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
19                heapq.heappush(pq, (dist[v], v))
20    
21    return dist

Template 2: Dijkstra with Constraints

1def dijkstra_constrained(n, edges, src, dst, k):
2    """Shortest path with at most k stops/constraints"""
3    graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
4    for u, v, w in edges:
5        graph[u].append((v, w))
6    
7    # (cost, node, stops_left)
8    pq = [(0, src, k + 1)]
9    # Track best stops count for each node
10    best = {}
11    
12    while pq:
13        cost, u, stops = heapq.heappop(pq)
14        
15        if u == dst:
16            return cost
17        
18        # Prune if we've seen this node with more stops
19        if u in best and best[u] >= stops:
20            continue
21        best[u] = stops
22        
23        if stops > 0:
24            for v, w in graph[u]:
25                heapq.heappush(pq, (cost + w, v, stops - 1))
26    
27    return -1

Template 3: Grid-based Dijkstra

1def dijkstra_grid(grid):
2    """Find minimum cost path in 2D grid"""
3    rows, cols = len(grid), len(grid[0])
4    
5    # Min heap: (cost, row, col)
6    pq = [(0, 0, 0)]
7    # Distance matrix
8    dist = [[float('inf')] * cols for _ in range(rows)]
9    dist[0][0] = 0
10    
11    directions = [(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)]
12    
13    while pq:
14        cost, r, c = heapq.heappop(pq)
15        
16        if r == rows - 1 and c == cols - 1:
17            return cost
18        
19        if cost > dist[r][c]:
20            continue
21        
22        for dr, dc in directions:
23            nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
24            if 0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols:
25                # Calculate new cost (problem-specific)
26                new_cost = max(cost, abs(grid[nr][nc] - grid[r][c]))
27                
28                if new_cost < dist[nr][nc]:
29                    dist[nr][nc] = new_cost
30                    heapq.heappush(pq, (new_cost, nr, nc))
31    
32    return -1

Template 4: Multi-Source Dijkstra

1def dijkstra_multi_source(n, edges, sources):
2    """Shortest paths from multiple sources"""
3    graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
4    for u, v, w in edges:
5        graph[u].append((v, w))
6    
7    dist = [float('inf')] * n
8    pq = []
9    
10    # Initialize all sources
11    for src in sources:
12        dist[src] = 0
13        heapq.heappush(pq, (0, src))
14    
15    while pq:
16        d, u = heapq.heappop(pq)
17        if d > dist[u]:
18            continue
19        
20        for v, w in graph[u]:
21            if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
22                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
23                heapq.heappush(pq, (dist[v], v))
24    
25    return dist

Template 5: Bidirectional Dijkstra

1def dijkstra_bidirectional(n, edges, src, dst):
2    """Optimize by searching from both ends"""
3    graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
4    reverse = collections.defaultdict(list)
5    for u, v, w in edges:
6        graph[u].append((v, w))
7        reverse[v].append((u, w))
8    
9    def dijkstra_helper(start, adj, other_dist):
10        dist = [float('inf')] * n
11        dist[start] = 0
12        pq = [(0, start)]
13        visited = set()
14        min_path = float('inf')
15        
16        while pq:
17            d, u = heapq.heappop(pq)
18            if u in visited:
19                continue
20            visited.add(u)
21            
22            # Check if we can form a complete path
23            if other_dist[u] != float('inf'):
24                min_path = min(min_path, d + other_dist[u])
25            
26            for v, w in adj[u]:
27                if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
28                    dist[v] = dist[u] + w
29                    heapq.heappush(pq, (dist[v], v))
30        
31        return dist, min_path
32    
33    # Run both directions
34    dist_fwd, path1 = dijkstra_helper(src, graph, [float('inf')] * n)
35    dist_bwd, path2 = dijkstra_helper(dst, reverse, dist_fwd)
36    
37    return min(path1, path2, dist_fwd[dst])

Problems by Pattern

Classic Shortest Path Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Network Delay Time 743 Basic Dijkstra Medium
Path with Maximum Probability 1514 Max-heap variant Medium
Find the City With Smallest Number 1334 All-pairs shortest path Medium
Minimum Weighted Subgraph 2203 Three sources Dijkstra Hard
Number of Ways to Arrive 1976 Count shortest paths Medium
Shortest Path in Binary Matrix 1091 Grid Dijkstra Medium

Constrained Path Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Cheapest Flights Within K Stops 787 State tracking Medium
Minimum Cost to Reach City 1928 K waypoints Hard
Shortest Path to Get All Keys 864 State bitmask Hard
Escape a Large Maze 1036 Limited BFS/Dijkstra Hard
Minimum Obstacle Removal 2290 0-1 BFS variant Hard

Grid-based Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Path With Minimum Effort 1631 Grid Dijkstra Medium
Swim in Rising Water 778 Min time path Hard
Minimum Cost to Make Valid Path 1368 Modified costs Hard
Shortest Path in a Grid 1293 K obstacles Hard
Trap Rain Water II 407 Priority queue Hard

Multi-Source Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Find Safest Path in Grid 2812 Multi-source init Medium
As Far from Land as Possible 1162 Multi-source BFS Medium
Shortest Distance from All Buildings 317 Multiple Dijkstra Hard
Minimum Height Trees 310 Center finding Medium

Time/State Dependent Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Second Minimum Time to Destination 2045 Track two values Hard
Reachable Nodes In Subdivided Graph 882 Edge subdivision Hard
Minimum Time to Visit All Points 2065 State tracking Hard
The Maze III 499 Lexicographic path Hard

LC Examples

2-1) Network Delay Time (LC 743) — Dijkstra Single Source

Dijkstra from source k; answer is max of all shortest distances, or -1 if any unreachable.

1// LC 743 - Network Delay Time
2// IDEA: Dijkstra from source k; max shortest dist = time for signal to reach all nodes
3// time = O((V+E) log V), space = O(V+E)
4public int networkDelayTime(int[][] times, int n, int k) {
5    Map<Integer, List<int[]>> graph = new HashMap<>();
6    for (int[] t : times) graph.computeIfAbsent(t[0], x -> new ArrayList<>()).add(new int[]{t[1], t[2]});
7    int[] dist = new int[n + 1];
8    Arrays.fill(dist, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
9    dist[k] = 0;
10    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
11    pq.offer(new int[]{0, k});
12    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
13        int[] cur = pq.poll();
14        int d = cur[0], u = cur[1];
15        if (d > dist[u]) continue;
16        for (int[] e : graph.getOrDefault(u, new ArrayList<>())) {
17            if (dist[u] + e[1] < dist[e[0]]) { dist[e[0]] = dist[u] + e[1]; pq.offer(new int[]{dist[e[0]], e[0]}); }
18        }
19    }
20    int max = 0;
21    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) { if (dist[i] == Integer.MAX_VALUE) return -1; max = Math.max(max, dist[i]); }
22    return max;
23}

1# LC 743 Network Delay Time
2# V0 
3# IDEA : Dijkstra
4class Solution:
5    def networkDelayTime(self, times, N, K):
6        K -= 1
7        nodes = collections.defaultdict(list)
8        for u, v, w in times:
9            nodes[u - 1].append((v - 1, w))
10        dist = [float('inf')] * N
11        dist[K] = 0
12        done = set()
13        for _ in range(N):
14            smallest = min((d, i) for (i, d) in enumerate(dist) if i not in done)[1]
15            for v, w in nodes[smallest]:
16                if v not in done and dist[smallest] + w < dist[v]:
17                    dist[v] = dist[smallest] + w
18            done.add(smallest)
19        return -1 if float('inf') in dist else max(dist)

2-2) Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (LC 787) — Dijkstra with Stop Counter

State = (cost, node, stops); skip if stops > k; don’t use visited set (stops matter).

1// LC 787 - Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
2// IDEA: Dijkstra with state (cost, node, stops); stop expanding when stops > k
3// time = O(E * K log(E*K)), space = O(E*K)
4public int findCheapestPrice(int n, int[][] flights, int src, int dst, int k) {
5    Map<Integer, List<int[]>> graph = new HashMap<>();
6    for (int[] f : flights) graph.computeIfAbsent(f[0], x -> new ArrayList<>()).add(new int[]{f[1], f[2]});
7    int[] dist = new int[n];
8    Arrays.fill(dist, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
9    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]); // [cost, node, stops]
10    pq.offer(new int[]{0, src, 0});
11    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
12        int[] cur = pq.poll();
13        int cost = cur[0], u = cur[1], stops = cur[2];
14        if (u == dst) return cost;
15        if (stops > k) continue;
16        for (int[] e : graph.getOrDefault(u, new ArrayList<>()))
17            if (cost + e[1] < dist[e[0]]) { dist[e[0]] = cost + e[1]; pq.offer(new int[]{dist[e[0]], e[0], stops + 1}); }
18    }
19    return -1;
20}

1# LC 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
2# V1
3# IDEA : Dijkstra
4# https://leetcode.com/problems/cheapest-flights-within-k-stops/discuss/267200/Python-Dijkstra
5# IDEA
6# -> To implement Dijkstra, we need a priority queue to pop out the lowest weight node for next search. In this case, the weight would be the accumulated flight cost. So my node takes a form of (cost, src, k). cost is the accumulated cost, src is the current node's location, k is stop times we left as we only have at most K stops. I also convert edges to an adjacent list based graph g.
7# -> Use a vis array to maintain visited nodes to avoid loop. vis[x] record the remaining steps to reach x with the lowest cost. If vis[x] >= k, then no need to visit that case (start from x with k steps left) as a better solution has been visited before (more remaining step and lower cost as heappopped beforehand). And we should initialize vis[x] to 0 to ensure visit always stop at a negative k.
8# -> Once k is used up (k == 0) or vis[x] >= k, we no longer push that node x to our queue. Once a popped cost is our destination, we get our lowest valid cost.
9# -> For Dijkstra, there is not need to maintain a best cost for each node since it's kind of greedy search. It always chooses the lowest cost node for next search. So the previous searched node always has a lower cost and has no chance to be updated. The first time we pop our destination from our queue, we have found the lowest cost to our destination.
10import collections
11import math
12class Solution:
13    def findCheapestPrice(self, n, flights, src, dst, K):
14        graph = collections.defaultdict(dict)
15        for s, d, w in flights:
16            graph[s][d] = w
17        pq = [(0, src, K+1)]
18        vis = [0] * n
19        while pq:
20            w, x, k = heapq.heappop(pq)
21            if x == dst:
22                return w
23            if vis[x] >= k:
24                continue
25            vis[x] = k
26            for y, dw in graph[x].items():
27                heapq.heappush(pq, (w+dw, y, k-1))
28        return -1

2-3) Path With Minimum Effort (LC 1631) — Dijkstra Min Effort on Grid

Minimize the maximum absolute difference along path; use min-heap with effort as priority key.

1// java
2// LC 1631
3
4// V0-1
5// IDEA: Dijkstra's ALGO ( fixed by gpt) : min PQ + BFS
6public int minimumEffortPath_0_1(int[][] heights) {
7    if (heights == null || heights.length == 0)
8        return 0;
9
10    int rows = heights.length;
11    int cols = heights[0].length;
12
13    // Min-heap: [effort, x, y]
14    PriorityQueue<int[]> minPQ = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingInt(a -> a[0]));
15    minPQ.offer(new int[] { 0, 0, 0 }); // effort, x, y
16
17    boolean[][] visited = new boolean[rows][cols];
18    int[][] directions = { { 0, 1 }, { 0, -1 }, { 1, 0 }, { -1, 0 } };
19
20    while (!minPQ.isEmpty()) {
21        int[] cur = minPQ.poll();
22        int effort = cur[0], x = cur[1], y = cur[2];
23
24        if (x == rows - 1 && y == cols - 1) {
25            return effort;
26        }
27
28  /**  NOTE !!!  need `visited, to NOT revisited visited cells (`Dijkstra algo`)
29   *
30   *   Reason:
31   *
32   *
33   *   Great question — and you’re absolutely right to raise this.
34   *
35   * ✅ Short Answer:
36   *
37   * Yes, in Dijkstra’s algorithm for the “minimum effort path” problem,
38   * we still need a visited check — but only after the shortest
39   * effort to a cell has been finalized.
40   *
41   * That is:
42   *    •   Once we’ve popped a cell (x, y) from the priority queue,
43   *        the effort it took to reach it is `guaranteed` to be `minimal`,
44   *        due to how the min-heap works.
45   *
46   *    •   After that point, there’s `NO need` to `revisit` that cell —
47   *        any future path that reaches (x, y) will have equal or greater effort,
48   *        and can be safely ignored.
49   *
50   * This is different from classic BFS where all edges are equal weight —
51   * but in Dijkstra, this greedy behavior is valid and optimal.
52   *
53   * ⸻
54   *
55   * 🤔 Why Not Revisit?
56   *
57   * Let’s break it down:
58   *
59   * In Dijkstra:
60   *    •   The min-heap (priority queue) guarantees that we always expand the least effort path so far.
61   *    •   If a cell is reached for the first time, it’s the best effort you’ll ever see to reach it.
62   *    •   If you allow revisiting, you’ll reprocess worse paths and slow down the algorithm.
63   *
64   * ⸻
65   *
66   * 📌 Exception:
67   *
68   * If you were doing plain BFS with no heap, or non-Dijkstra variants,
69   * you’d need to revisit when a better cost is found later (like in Bellman-Ford).
70   * But with Dijkstra and a correct min-heap structure,
71   * no revisits are necessary after finalization.
72   *
73   * ⸻
74   *
75   * ✅ Key Rule:
76   *
77   * In Dijkstra:
78   * Once you pop a node (x, y) from the min-heap and mark it visited,
79   * you do not need to revisit it — its shortest (or in this case, minimum effort) path is finalized.
80   *
81   */
82  if (visited[x][y]) {
83            continue;
84        }
85
86        visited[x][y] = true;
87
88        for (int[] dir : directions) {
89            int nx = x + dir[0];
90            int ny = y + dir[1];
91
92            if (nx >= 0 && ny >= 0 && nx < rows && ny < cols && !visited[nx][ny]) {
93                int newEffort = Math.max(effort, Math.abs(heights[nx][ny] - heights[x][y]));
94                minPQ.offer(new int[] { newEffort, nx, ny });
95            }
96        }
97    }
98
99    return -1; // Should never reach here if input is valid
100}

2-4) Path with Maximum Probability (LC 1514) — Dijkstra Max-Heap on Probabilities

Max-heap Dijkstra multiplying edge probabilities; start at 1.0, maximize reach-probability.

1// java
2// LC 1514
3// IDEA: Modified Dijkstra (max-heap, multiply probabilities instead of adding distances)
4// NOTE: Use MAX heap since we want maximum probability
5// NOTE: Initialize probabilities to -1 (unreachable), source to 1
6class Solution {
7    public double maxProbability(int n, int[][] edges, double[] succProb, int start, int end) {
8        List<double[]>[] graph = new LinkedList[n];
9        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
10            graph[i] = new LinkedList<>();
11        }
12        for (int i = 0; i < edges.length; i++) {
13            graph[edges[i][0]].add(new double[]{edges[i][1], succProb[i]});
14            graph[edges[i][1]].add(new double[]{edges[i][0], succProb[i]});
15        }
16
17        double[] proTo = new double[n];
18        Arrays.fill(proTo, -1);
19        proTo[start] = 1;
20
21        // NOTE: MAX heap (compare b vs a)
22        PriorityQueue<double[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> Double.compare(b[1], a[1]));
23        pq.offer(new double[]{start, 1});
24
25        while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
26            double[] cur = pq.poll();
27            int curId = (int) cur[0];
28            double curProb = cur[1];
29
30            if (curId == end) return curProb;
31            if (proTo[curId] > curProb) continue;
32
33            for (double[] next : graph[curId]) {
34                int nextId = (int) next[0];
35                double newProb = proTo[curId] * next[1];
36                if (newProb > proTo[nextId]) {
37                    proTo[nextId] = newProb;
38                    pq.offer(new double[]{nextId, newProb});
39                }
40            }
41        }
42        return 0;
43    }
44}

1# python
2# LC 1514
3# IDEA: Modified Dijkstra with max-heap (negate probability for max behavior)
4import heapq
5import collections
6
7class Solution:
8    def maxProbability(self, n, edges, succProb, start_node, end_node):
9        graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
10        for i, (u, v) in enumerate(edges):
11            graph[u].append((v, succProb[i]))
12            graph[v].append((u, succProb[i]))
13
14        # Max-heap: negate probability since heapq is min-heap
15        pq = [(-1.0, start_node)]
16        dist = [0.0] * n
17        dist[start_node] = 1.0
18
19        while pq:
20            neg_prob, u = heapq.heappop(pq)
21            prob = -neg_prob
22
23            if u == end_node:
24                return prob
25            if prob < dist[u]:
26                continue
27
28            for v, w in graph[u]:
29                new_prob = prob * w
30                if new_prob > dist[v]:
31                    dist[v] = new_prob
32                    heapq.heappush(pq, (-new_prob, v))
33
34        return 0.0

2-5) Number of Ways to Arrive at Destination (LC 1976) — Dijkstra + Path Count

Standard Dijkstra; track count of shortest paths at each node alongside minimum distance.

1// java
2// LC 1976
3// IDEA: Dijkstra + count paths
4// NOTE: Track both shortest distance AND number of ways to reach each node
5class Solution {
6    public int countPaths(int n, int[][] roads) {
7        int MOD = 1_000_000_007;
8        List<long[]>[] graph = new ArrayList[n];
9        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) graph[i] = new ArrayList<>();
10
11        for (int[] r : roads) {
12            graph[r[0]].add(new long[]{r[1], r[2]});
13            graph[r[1]].add(new long[]{r[0], r[2]});
14        }
15
16        long[] dist = new long[n];
17        long[] ways = new long[n];
18        Arrays.fill(dist, Long.MAX_VALUE);
19        dist[0] = 0;
20        ways[0] = 1;
21
22        // (distance, node)
23        PriorityQueue<long[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingLong(a -> a[0]));
24        pq.offer(new long[]{0, 0});
25
26        while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
27            long[] cur = pq.poll();
28            long d = cur[0];
29            int u = (int) cur[1];
30
31            if (d > dist[u]) continue;
32
33            for (long[] next : graph[u]) {
34                int v = (int) next[0];
35                long w = next[1];
36
37                if (dist[u] + w < dist[v]) {
38                    dist[v] = dist[u] + w;
39                    ways[v] = ways[u];
40                    pq.offer(new long[]{dist[v], v});
41                } else if (dist[u] + w == dist[v]) {
42                    ways[v] = (ways[v] + ways[u]) % MOD;
43                }
44            }
45        }
46
47        return (int) (ways[n - 1] % MOD);
48    }
49}

1# python
2# LC 1976
3# IDEA: Dijkstra + count shortest paths
4import heapq
5import collections
6
7class Solution:
8    def countPaths(self, n, roads):
9        MOD = 10**9 + 7
10        graph = collections.defaultdict(list)
11        for u, v, w in roads:
12            graph[u].append((v, w))
13            graph[v].append((u, w))
14
15        dist = [float('inf')] * n
16        ways = [0] * n
17        dist[0] = 0
18        ways[0] = 1
19        pq = [(0, 0)]  # (distance, node)
20
21        while pq:
22            d, u = heapq.heappop(pq)
23            if d > dist[u]:
24                continue
25            for v, w in graph[u]:
26                if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
27                    dist[v] = dist[u] + w
28                    ways[v] = ways[u]
29                    heapq.heappush(pq, (dist[v], v))
30                elif dist[u] + w == dist[v]:
31                    ways[v] = (ways[v] + ways[u]) % MOD
32
33        return ways[n - 1] % MOD

2-6) Swim in Rising Water (LC 778) — Dijkstra Min-Max on Grid

Min-heap where priority = max elevation seen so far; answer = time to reach bottom-right.

1// java
2// LC 778
3// IDEA: Dijkstra (min PQ + BFS on grid)
4// NOTE: Track max elevation along path (not sum of weights)
5public int swimInWater(int[][] grid) {
6    int n = grid.length;
7    PriorityQueue<int[]> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingInt(a -> a[0]));
8    boolean[][] visited = new boolean[n][n];
9
10    minHeap.offer(new int[]{grid[0][0], 0, 0});
11    visited[0][0] = true;
12
13    int[][] directions = {{0, 1}, {0, -1}, {1, 0}, {-1, 0}};
14    int res = 0;
15
16    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
17        int[] cur = minHeap.poll();
18        int elevation = cur[0], x = cur[1], y = cur[2];
19
20        // NOTE: track MAX elevation along path
21        res = Math.max(res, elevation);
22
23        if (x == n - 1 && y == n - 1) return res;
24
25        for (int[] d : directions) {
26            int nx = x + d[0], ny = y + d[1];
27            if (nx >= 0 && ny >= 0 && nx < n && ny < n && !visited[ny][nx]) {
28                visited[ny][nx] = true;
29                minHeap.offer(new int[]{grid[ny][nx], nx, ny});
30            }
31        }
32    }
33    return -1;
34}

2-7) Trapping Rain Water II (LC 407) — Multi-Source Dijkstra from Boundary

Process boundary cells with min-heap; water trapped = max(boundary height) - cell height.

1// java
2// LC 407
3// IDEA: Multi-source Dijkstra (PQ from boundary inward)
4// NOTE: Start from all boundary cells, expand inward with min-heap
5// NOTE: Water trapped at a cell = max(0, boundary_height - cell_height)
6public int trapRainWater(int[][] heightMap) {
7    if (heightMap == null || heightMap.length < 3 || heightMap[0].length < 3)
8        return 0;
9
10    int rows = heightMap.length, cols = heightMap[0].length;
11    boolean[][] visited = new boolean[rows][cols];
12    // PQ: [height, row, col]
13    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingInt(a -> a[0]));
14
15    // Push all border cells
16    for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) {
17        pq.offer(new int[]{heightMap[0][c], 0, c});
18        pq.offer(new int[]{heightMap[rows - 1][c], rows - 1, c});
19        visited[0][c] = true;
20        visited[rows - 1][c] = true;
21    }
22    for (int r = 1; r < rows - 1; r++) {
23        pq.offer(new int[]{heightMap[r][0], r, 0});
24        pq.offer(new int[]{heightMap[r][cols - 1], r, cols - 1});
25        visited[r][0] = true;
26        visited[r][cols - 1] = true;
27    }
28
29    int totalWater = 0;
30    int[][] dirs = {{-1, 0}, {1, 0}, {0, -1}, {0, 1}};
31
32    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
33        int[] cell = pq.poll();
34        for (int[] d : dirs) {
35            int nr = cell[1] + d[0], nc = cell[2] + d[1];
36            if (nr < 0 || nr >= rows || nc < 0 || nc >= cols || visited[nr][nc])
37                continue;
38            visited[nr][nc] = true;
39            int h = heightMap[nr][nc];
40            if (h < cell[0]) {
41                totalWater += cell[0] - h;
42                pq.offer(new int[]{cell[0], nr, nc}); // raise to boundary level
43            } else {
44                pq.offer(new int[]{h, nr, nc});
45            }
46        }
47    }
48    return totalWater;
49}

2-8) Minimum Obstacle Removal to Reach Corner (LC 2290) — 0-1 BFS / Dijkstra

Cost = 1 for obstacle, 0 for empty cell; use 0-1 BFS (deque) or Dijkstra to minimize total.

1// java
2// LC 2290
3
4// V0-1
5// IDEA: Dijkstra's Algorithm (fixed by gpt)
6/**
7 *  NOTE !!!
8 *
9 * ✅ Summary:
10 *  •   Single cost var won’t work → need dist[][] to track per-cell minimum cost.
11 *  •   No explicit visited needed → the dist[][] + early skip (if (cost > dist[y][x]) continue) handles that.
12 *
13 */
14public int minimumObstacles(int[][] grid) {
15    if (grid == null || grid.length == 0 || grid[0].length == 0) {
16        return 0;
17    }
18
19    int m = grid.length; // rows
20    int n = grid[0].length; // cols
21
22    /**
23     *   NOTE !!!
24     *
25     *    we need a 2D array to save the cost when BFS loop over the grid
26     *    (CAN'T just use a single var (cost))
27     *
28     * ---
29     *
30     * 1. Why keep a dist[][] array instead of a single cost variable?
31     *
32     *
33     *  •   The minimum cost to reach a cell (x,y) is not unique across the grid.
34     *  •   For example, you might reach (2,2) with cost 3 via one path, but later find a better path with cost 2.
35     *  •   If you only had a single global cost variable, you couldn’t distinguish the costs of different cells — you’d lose information.
36     *
37     * That’s why:
38     *  •   dist[y][x] keeps track of the best cost found so far for each specific cell.
39     *  •   Dijkstra works by always expanding the lowest-cost node next, and updating neighbors only if we find a cheaper path.
40     *
41     * Without dist[][], you’d either:
42     *  •   Revisit nodes unnecessarily (potential infinite loops), or
43     *  •   Miss better paths (return wrong result).
44     */
45    // distance[y][x] = min obstacles to reach (y,x)
46    int[][] dist = new int[m][n];
47    for (int[] row : dist) {
48        Arrays.fill(row, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
49    }
50    dist[0][0] = 0;
51
52    // PQ stores [cost, x, y]
53    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingInt(a -> a[0]));
54    pq.offer(new int[] { 0, 0, 0 }); // start at (0,0) with cost=0
55
56    int[][] moves = { { 1, 0 }, { -1, 0 }, { 0, 1 }, { 0, -1 } };
57
58    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
59        int[] cur = pq.poll();
60        int cost = cur[0], x = cur[1], y = cur[2];
61
62        // Reached destination
63        if (x == n - 1 && y == m - 1) {
64            return cost;
65        }
66
67        // Skip if we already found better
68        /**
69         *  NOTE !!!
70         *
71         *   why DON'T need to maintain a `visited` var
72         *   to prevent repeating visit ?
73         *
74         *  -----
75         *
76         *   2. Why no explicit visited array?
77         *
78         * This is subtle. In Dijkstra:
79         *  •   A node is considered “visited” (finalized) once it’s dequeued from the priority queue with its minimum cost.
80         *  •   Because of the if (cost > dist[y][x]) continue; check, we automatically ignore revisits that don’t improve cost.
81         *
82         *
83         *  So, the role of visited is effectively played by:
84         *
85         *      ```
86         *      if (cost > dist[y][x]) continue;
87         *      ```
88         *
89         *   This guarantees:
90         *  •   The first time you pop a cell with its minimum cost, you expand it.
91         *  •   If another path later tries to reach the same cell with a higher cost, it gets ignored.
92         *
93         * 👉 That’s why visited isn’t needed in Dijkstra — the dist[][] array + priority queue ensure correctness.
94         *
95         */
96        if (cost > dist[y][x])
97            continue;
98
99        for (int[] mv : moves) {
100            int nx = x + mv[0];
101            int ny = y + mv[1];
102
103            if (nx >= 0 && nx < n && ny >= 0 && ny < m) {
104                int newCost = cost + grid[ny][nx];
105                if (newCost < dist[ny][nx]) {
106                    dist[ny][nx] = newCost;
107                    pq.offer(new int[] { newCost, nx, ny });
108                }
109            }
110        }
111    }
112
113    return -1; // should never happen
114}

Decision Framework

Pattern Selection Strategy

1Dijkstra Algorithm Selection Flowchart:
2
31. Is it a shortest path problem?
4   ├── NO → Consider other algorithms (DFS, BFS, DP)
5   └── YES → Continue to 2
6
72. Are all edge weights non-negative?
8   ├── NO → Use Bellman-Ford or SPFA
9   └── YES → Continue to 3
10
113. Single source or multiple sources?
12   ├── Multiple → Use Multi-Source Dijkstra (Template 4)
13   └── Single → Continue to 4
14
154. Is it on a graph or grid?
16   ├── Grid → Use Grid-based Dijkstra (Template 3)
17   └── Graph → Continue to 5
18
195. Any constraints (K stops, budget, time)?
20   ├── YES → Use Constrained Dijkstra (Template 2)
21   └── NO → Use Basic Dijkstra (Template 1)
22
236. Need optimization for large graphs?
24   ├── YES → Consider Bidirectional Dijkstra (Template 5)
25   └── NO → Use selected template from above

When to Use Dijkstra vs BFS

Criteria Dijkstra BFS
Edge weights Non-negative, varying All equal (unweighted) or 0/1
Data structure Priority Queue (min-heap) Queue (LinkedList)
Time complexity O((V + E) log V) O(V + E)
First visit = shortest? ❌ No (must relax via PQ) ✅ Yes (level = distance)
“Minimum cost/weight” ✅ Use Dijkstra ❌ Wrong answer
“Minimum steps/moves” ❌ Overkill ✅ Use BFS
Grid with varying costs ✅ Dijkstra on implicit graph ❌
Grid with uniform cost ❌ Unnecessary overhead ✅ BFS

Decision rule: If every edge has the same cost (or cost is 1), use BFS — it’s simpler and O(V+E). The moment edges have different non-negative weights, use Dijkstra.

Common trap: Using Dijkstra (PQ) for problems like LC 279 Perfect Squares or LC 752 Open the Lock where all edges cost 1 — plain BFS is sufficient and faster.

0-1 BFS special case: If edges are weighted 0 or 1 only, use a deque — push weight-0 edges to front, weight-1 edges to back. O(V+E) like BFS, handles two weights correctly. Example: LC 2290 Minimum Obstacle Removal.

When to Use Dijkstra vs Other Algorithms

Scenario Use Dijkstra Use Alternative Alternative Algorithm
Negative weights ❌ ✅ Bellman-Ford
Unweighted graph ❌ ✅ BFS
All-pairs shortest path ❌ ✅ Floyd-Warshall
Single source, non-negative ✅ ❌ -
Need path reconstruction ✅ - Track parent nodes
Dense graphs ⚠️ Consider Bellman-Ford
Sparse graphs ✅ ❌ -

Algorithm Comparison: Dijkstra vs Floyd-Warshall vs Bellman-Ford

Comprehensive Comparison Table

Feature Dijkstra Floyd-Warshall Bellman-Ford
Problem Type Single-source shortest path All-pairs shortest path Single-source shortest path
Time Complexity O((V+E) log V) with heap O(V³) O(V·E)
Space Complexity O(V) O(V²) O(V)
Negative Weights ❌ No ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
Negative Cycles N/A Detects Detects
Implementation Moderate (priority queue) Very simple (3 loops) Simple (2 loops)
Data Structure Min-heap/Priority Queue 2D matrix Edge list + distance array
Graph Type Best for sparse graphs Best for dense graphs Works with any
Output Distances from one source All-pairs distances Distances from one source
Early Termination ✅ Can stop at target ❌ Must complete ❌ Must run V-1 iterations
Best Use Case Large sparse graphs, single-source Small complete graphs, all-pairs Negative weights, cycle detection
Worst Case Graph Dense graphs Very large graphs Dense graphs with many edges

When to Use Each Algorithm

1Shortest Path Algorithm Selection:
2
31. What type of problem?
4   ├── All-pairs shortest path? → Continue to 2
5   │   ├── Small graph (V ≤ 400)? → Use Floyd-Warshall
6   │   └── Large graph? → Run Dijkstra V times (or Johnson's algorithm)
7   │
8   └── Single-source shortest path? → Continue to 3
9
102. Are edge weights non-negative?
11   ├── YES → Use Dijkstra (most efficient)
12   │   ├── Sparse graph? → Dijkstra with binary heap: O((V+E) log V)
13   │   └── Dense graph? → Consider array-based: O(V²)
14   │
15   └── NO (has negative weights) → Use Bellman-Ford
16       └── Need cycle detection? → Bellman-Ford explicitly detects
17
183. Special cases:
19   ├── Unweighted graph? → Use BFS: O(V+E)
20   ├── Tree structure? → Use DFS/BFS: O(V)
21   ├── Grid-based? → Dijkstra on implicit graph
22   └── Transitive closure? → Floyd-Warshall (boolean variant)

Practical Comparison Examples

Example 1: Social Network (1000 users, 5000 friendships)

  • Single-source (find distances from one user):

    • Dijkstra: ~5000 × log(1000) ≈ 50,000 operations ⚡ Best choice
    • Bellman-Ford: 1000 × 5000 = 5,000,000 operations
    • Floyd-Warshall: 1000³ = 1,000,000,000 operations

  • All-pairs (distances between all users):

    • Dijkstra × V: 50,000 × 1000 = 50,000,000 operations ⚡ Best choice
    • Floyd-Warshall: 1,000,000,000 operations (simpler code)

Example 2: Small Complete Graph (50 nodes, fully connected)

  • All-pairs shortest paths:
    • Floyd-Warshall: 50³ = 125,000 operations ⚡ Best choice (simplest)
    • Dijkstra × V: ~2500 × log(50) × 50 = ~500,000 operations

Example 3: Currency Exchange with Arbitrage Detection

  • Detect negative cycles (arbitrage opportunities):
    • Bellman-Ford: O(V·E) ⚡ Best choice (explicitly detects)
    • Floyd-Warshall: O(V³), checks diagonal (works for all-pairs)
    • Dijkstra: ❌ Cannot handle negative weights

Performance Benchmarks

Graph Size Edges Dijkstra (single) Dijkstra (all-pairs) Floyd-Warshall Bellman-Ford
V=100, Sparse 500 0.01ms 1ms 10ms ⚡ 5ms
V=100, Dense 5000 0.1ms 10ms ⚡ 10ms 50ms
V=500, Sparse 2500 0.05ms 25ms ⚡ 1.25s 125ms
V=500, Dense 125K 2ms 1s 1.25s ⚡ 6.25s
V=1000, Sparse 5000 0.1ms 100ms ⚡ 10s 500ms

(Times are approximate, assuming optimized implementations)

Algorithm Selection Matrix

Your Situation Recommended Algorithm Why
Need shortest path from A to B in road network Dijkstra Single-source, non-negative, can stop early
Find center of small network (≤300 nodes) Floyd-Warshall Need all-pairs, small graph, simple code
Route planning in city with traffic (dynamic costs) Dijkstra (re-run) Real-time updates, single-source
Check if prerequisite chain exists Floyd-Warshall Transitive closure, small graph
Currency arbitrage detection Bellman-Ford Negative cycle detection needed
Social network - degrees of separation BFS (if unweighted) Unweighted, single-source
Minimum spanning tree Prim’s/Kruskal’s Different problem entirely
Game pathfinding on grid Dijkstra or A* Sparse grid, heuristic available

Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Implementation Time Complexity Space Complexity Notes
Array-based O(V²) O(V) Good for dense graphs
Binary Heap O((V+E)logV) O(V) Most common
Fibonacci Heap O(E + VlogV) O(V) Theoretical best
Grid-based O(RC log(RC)) O(RC) R=rows, C=cols

Template Quick Reference

Template Best For Key Code Pattern
Basic Standard shortest path heapq.heappop(pq) → relax edges
Constrained K-stops, budget limits Track state: (cost, node, constraint)
Grid 2D matrix problems 4-directional movement
Multi-Source Multiple starting points Initialize all sources
Bidirectional Large graphs Search from both ends

Common Patterns & Tricks

Priority Queue State

1# Basic state
2(distance, node)
3
4# With constraints
5(cost, node, stops_remaining)
6
7# Grid problems
8(cost, row, col)
9
10# With path tracking
11(distance, node, path)

Visited Set Optimization

1# Option 1: Check after pop (recommended)
2if node in visited:
3    continue
4visited.add(node)
5
6# Option 2: Check distance
7if d > dist[node]:
8    continue

Path Reconstruction

1parent = {}
2# During relaxation:
3parent[v] = u
4
5# Reconstruct path:
6path = []
7while node != source:
8    path.append(node)
9    node = parent[node]
10path.reverse()

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify graph structure: Explicit edges or implicit (grid)?
  2. Check constraints: Non-negative weights? Single source?
  3. Choose template: Basic, constrained, grid, or multi-source?
  4. Define state: What needs tracking in priority queue?
  5. Implement relaxation: How to update distances?
  6. Handle termination: When to stop? Return what value?

Similar LeetCode Problems Reference

Grid-Based Problems

LC # Title Movement Key Feature Primary Approach Alt Approaches dist[][] Needed?
64 Minimum Path Sum ↓→ only Additive cost 2D DP 1D DP ❌ No
1631 Path With Minimum Effort 4-dir Max step diff (non-additive) Dijkstra Binary Search, Union Find ✅ Yes
778 Swim in Rising Water 4-dir Max grid value Dijkstra Union Find ✅ Yes
1263 Minimum Moves to Move Box 4-dir Push box mechanics Dijkstra + state - ✅ Yes
882 Reachable Nodes In Subdivided Graph Graph Node subdivision Dijkstra - ✅ Yes

LC 1631 Deep Dive:

  • Solutions Available: 4 major approaches (Dijkstra dist[], Dijkstra visited, Binary Search, Union Find)
  • Most Common: Dijkstra with dist[][] array or visited[] array
  • Key Insight: The cost model is Math.max(effort, step_diff), not additive—this makes DP impossible
  • Reference: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/Graph/PathWithMinimumEffort.java (V0-V4.3)

Classic Shortest Path Problems

LC # Title Type Key Feature
743 Network Delay Time Graph Broadcast delays
787 Cheapest Flights K Stops Graph K-stop constraint
1514 Path with Maximum Probability Graph Maximize probability
1928 Minimum Cost to Reach Destination Weighted Graph K waypoints

Multi-Source Shortest Path

LC # Title Key Feature
1162 As Far from Land as Possible Multi-source BFS-Dijkstra
2812 Find the Safest Path Grid-based multi-source
2290 Minimum Obstacle Removal 0-1 BFS variant

Key Implementation Files

  • Java Reference: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/DynamicProgramming/MinimumPathSum.java
    • V0: Dijkstra with dist[][] (works but overkill)
    • V0-0-1, V1, V2: Pure DP approaches (optimal for LC 64)

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Forgetting to check if already visited
  • Using Dijkstra with negative weights
  • Not using priority queue (using regular queue)
  • Incorrect state comparison in constrained problems
  • Not handling disconnected components

✅ Best Practices:

  • Always use min-heap for priority queue
  • Track visited nodes to avoid reprocessing
  • Initialize distances to infinity except source
  • Consider using distance array vs visited set
  • Handle edge cases (empty graph, no path)

Interview Tips

  1. Clarify constraints: Always ask about negative weights
  2. State complexity: Mention time/space complexity upfront
  3. Explain relaxation: Core concept of updating distances
  4. Consider alternatives: Mention when BFS or Bellman-Ford better
  5. Optimize if needed: Discuss bidirectional search for large graphs

Related Topics

  • BFS: Unweighted shortest path
  • Bellman-Ford: Handles negative weights (see detailed comparison above)
  • Floyd-Warshall: All-pairs shortest path (see detailed comparison above)
  • A Algorithm*: Heuristic-guided search
  • SPFA: Queue-optimized Bellman-Ford variant
  • Johnson’s Algorithm: All-pairs with reweighting technique
